retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizure(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Generalized myoclonic seizures: Bilateral, synchronous jerks affecting the upper and lower extremities. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Myoclonic Epilepsy of Infancy -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Myoclonic seizures are the only type of seizures occurring in MEI. MEI is characterized by myoclonic jerks that occur as either a brief solitary episode (1 to 2 secs) or a cluster of 2 to 8  involving predominantly the head and upper extremities. Myoclonic jerks are rapid, isolated, but simultaneous synchronous jerks of various muscle groups. Myoclonic seizures are often spontaneous, frequently occurring in wakefulness, less often during drowsiness or slow-wave sleep. The following features are noted in MEI: Myoclonic jerks involving upper extremities Head nodding, upward rolling of eyes, blinking Falls if standing with lower extremities involved Yelling or cough-like vocalization due to sudden diaphragmatic contraction (may sometimes accompany the myoclonic jerks) Consciousness is usually intact (mild alteration of consciousness can accompany a cluster of myoclonic seizures) [14] [15] The following variants of MEI have been reported:

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizure(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Nonepileptic behaviors should be carefully distinguished from neonatal seizures. [56] These movements can closely resemble epileptic events. Thus, EEG is recommended for accurate characterization. [57] Normal neonatal behaviors that may mimic seizures include sucking motions, hiccuping, and benign neonatal sleep myoclonus, which is physiologic myoclonic activity that occurs during sleep. Other nonepileptic movements, often observed in neonates with systemic illness, include jitteriness, apnea, clonus, tremors, and exaggerated startle responses, such as hyperekplexia. [58] [59]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizure(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Myoclonic seizures: Abrupt, nonrepetitive muscle contractions that typically involve the flexor muscle groups of the upper extremities, trunk, diaphragm, or face.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizure(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Focal clonic seizures: Present with repetitive, rhythmic contractions involving the face, limbs, neck, or trunk. These movements cannot be suppressed by repositioning or physical restraint. Seizure activity may migrate during the same ictal event, most commonly to the contralateral hemisphere, though ipsilateral migration can also occur.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizure(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Focal tonic seizures: Involve sustained, transient posturing of an extremity, the trunk, or the neck. These seizures may be associated with horizontal eye deviation.

retrieved_docs.title: Infantile Epileptic Spasms Syndrome (West Syndrome) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients are grouped into symptomatic versus cryptogenic versus idiopathic infantile spasms, but clinicians must be able first to identify the clinical features that prompt further investigation for a diagnosis. As stated above, infantile spasms are characterized by epileptic spasms with onset in infancy or early childhood that are usually associated with the EEG pattern of hypsarrhythmia and developmental regression. [1] As the name indicates, 90% of children affected by infantile spasms present at less than 1 year of age with a peak incidence of 3 to 7 months. [14] [16] Furthermore, as the name indicates, infantile spasms are defined by spasms that involve the muscles of the neck, trunk, and extremities; spasms may be flexor, extensor, or mixed flexor-extensor. [14] Clinicians may note movements such as head bobbing or body crunching. The spasms typically occur in 2 phases; the initial phase is sudden onset, lasting less than 2 seconds, with brief contractions of 1 or more muscle groups. This is followed by a less intense, longer tonic phase lasting 2 to 10 seconds. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizures and Neonatal Epilepsy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Other symptoms that may mimic seizures include motor automatisms such as repetitive eye opening, eye deviation, tongue movements, or tonic posturing. Patients exhibiting these symptoms should be thoroughly evaluated for underlying nervous system pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizures and Neonatal Epilepsy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Physiological movements in normal newborns that may mimic seizures include neonatal tremors, jitteriness, tremulousness, hyperekplexia, and benign neonatal sleep myoclonus. [35] Notably, benign neonatal sleep myoclonus can be differentiated from seizures because the myoclonic movements cease when the neonate awakens.

retrieved_docs.title: EEG Neonatal Visual Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Classification
retrieved_docs.content: Subtle: (approximately 50%) Difficult to detect clinically. It can consist of aleatory roving eye movements, including open fluttering and rolling up. Oral smacking or automatisms, pedal movements, staring/apneic events, repetitive sucking, chewing sounds, and tongue protrusion. Clonic: (approximately 25%)  Involuntary, rhythmic muscle contractions that are fast frequency. Myoclonic: (approximately 20%) Repetitive brief shock-like jerks of the limbs. Tonic: (approximately 5%) Repetitive and sustained tonic/stiffening flexion or extension of the limbs, with decerebrate and decorticate like posturing. Generalized tonic-clonic: Repetitive alternating tonic-clonic movements that represent a primary or secondarily generalized seizure associated with poor prognosis, but very rare.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizure(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Generalized clonic seizures: Characterized by diffuse, bilateral, and synchronous motor activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Infantile Spasm (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Infantile spasms are epileptic spasms that occur in infancy or early childhood. These spasms are classically characterized clinically by symmetric, brief jerking spells that involve the head, neck, arms, legs, and abdomen which may consist of flexion, extension, or a combination of flexion-extension. Infantile spasms often are associated with a characteristic pattern on electroencephalogram (EEG) called hypsarrhythmia. When the triad of infantile spasms, hypsarrhythmia, and developmental regression occur together, this triad is termed West Syndrome. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizure(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Generalized tonic seizures: May mimic decerebrate (extension of both upper and lower extremities) or decorticate (flexion of the upper extremities with extension of the lower extremities) posturing. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Seizures and Neonatal Epilepsy -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Sandifer syndrome in neonates can also be mistaken for seizures. This syndrome typically presents with paroxysmal head and neck spasms, often sparing the limbs, along with back arching. This syndrome is associated with gastroesophageal reflux. Reassuring the parents is essential in these cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Infantile Spasm (Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Children with infantile spasms experience brief jerking spells of flexion, extension, or combination of flexion-extension of the head, neck, arms, legs, and trunk that usually last a few seconds or less. These spasms often occur in clusters and clusters often occur during sleep-wake transitions. Spasms also are seen more frequently during waking hours than during sleep. Patients return to a baseline mental state within seconds after the spasm. Some children may appear scared or upset by the clusters of spasm activity. Evidence of developmental delay or regression may be apparent on history and physical exam, although patients who come to medical attention shortly after the onset of spasms may still be developmentally normal. Otherwise, a physical exam is often unremarkable, although a careful skin exam is necessary to screen for possible underlying genetic disorders such as neurocutaneous disorders known to cause infantile spasms. Tuberous sclerosis is one such neurocutaneous disorder that is often associated with infantile spasms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1440
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all classified as اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, a neonatal disorder. This grouping indicates that both forms of transient neonatal diabetes mellitus and the syndrome that combines developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes fall under the broader category of neonatal disorders, highlighting their importance in newborn assessment, monitoring, and specialized management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2520
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma is recognized as a specific form of newborn disorder (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة), representing a narrower subset within the broader category of neonatal disorders. At the same time, it is classified as a particular manifestation of birth trauma, indicating that this cerebral injury falls under the general umbrella of injuries caused by traumatic events during delivery. Understanding that cerebral injury due to birth trauma is both a distinct neonatal disorder and a type of birth trauma helps clinicians identify, assess, and manage these injuries more effectively in newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma and DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) are both classified as specific forms of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, the broader category of neonatal disorders. The condition Cerebral injury due to birth trauma represents a narrower, trauma‑related neurological injury that occurs in the newborn period, while transient neonatal diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disorder that appears shortly after birth and typically resolves. Recognizing that both entities fall under the umbrella of neonatal disorders highlights the need for targeted assessment and management strategies tailored to the unique pathophysiology of each condition in newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1089
retrieved_docs.content: with or lead to infectious complications around birth. Overall, the knowledge network portrays neonatal disorders as an interconnected group of conditions that include traumatic cerebral injury, intracranial and neonatal hemorrhage, perinatal infections, metabolic diseases such as transient neonatal diabetes, neurologic problems like developmental delay and epilepsy, and extreme prematurity. Birth trauma is a key antecedent of intracranial bleeding, which is classified under neonatal hemorrhage, while perinatal infection serves both as a parent condition for unspecified perinatal disorders and as a member of the broader neonatal disorder set. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns presenting with any of these interrelated pathologies.

Recognizing this relationship highlights the importance of evaluating neurological complications when assessing and managing birth‑related injuries.

This mapping underscores that transient neonatal diabetes mellitus and the syndrome combining developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes represent related early‑life metabolic and neurological conditions, highlighting the need for integrated recognition and management of such disorders in newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2790
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all mapped to the broader condition مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). These specific neonatal and developmental presentations are therefore recognized as manifestations or subtypes of diabetes mellitus. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, indicating that retinal disorders such as diabetic retinopathy commonly occur in patients with diabetes mellitus. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians identify atypical early‑onset forms of diabetes and monitor for ocular complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 683
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease that is linked to a wide range of clinical findings, genetic factors, therapeutic considerations, and related disorders. Various forms of neonatal diabetes, including Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 2 (disorder), and the syndrome of Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to diabetes, highlighting its relevance from birth onward. Genetic dysfunction of the human INSR protein is specifically associated with diabetes, underscoring a molecular basis for the disease. Clinically, diabetes is associated with several severe complications of the lower extremities. Amputation of lower extremity includes child procedures such as toe amputation (بتر أصبع القدم) and below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة), both of which are clinically associated with diabetes. Necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb is an associated morphology (النَخَر) and is linked

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2509
retrieved_docs.content: Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are collectively mapped to the neonatal disorder described by the Arabic term اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, indicating that this combination of neurodevelopmental delay, seizure activity, and early‑onset diabetes is recognized as a specific disorder of the newborn. Likewise, Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1—referring to transient neonatal diabetes mellitus—is also mapped to اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, showing that this temporary form of diabetes in the newborn falls under the same category of neonatal disorder. Together, these mappings underscore that both the triad of developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes and transient neonatal diabetes mellitus represent manifestations of a disorder occurring in the newborn period, emphasizing the importance of early identification and integrated management in neonatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 509
retrieved_docs.content: developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes within the same diagnostic framework as other neonatal disorders. Overall, the knowledge network connects perinatal insults—such as birth trauma, intracerebral hemorrhage, and foetal undernutrition—to a continuum of disorders ranging from metabolic (transient neonatal diabetes) to neurological (developmental delay, epilepsy, cerebral injury) conditions in infants and newborns. Recognizing these interrelationships is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient care in the neonatal period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1740
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis/Diseases Component is classified under مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). The specific disorders DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are both mapped to مرض السكري, indicating they are forms of transient neonatal diabetes that fall within the broader category of diabetes mellitus. The combined condition Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes is also mapped to مرض السكري, highlighting its association with neonatal diabetes as part of the diabetes spectrum. Clinically, اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder) is associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Additionally, متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome) is identified as a child of مرض السكري, signifying that metabolic syndrome can arise as a downstream metabolic complication of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate how various neonatal diabetes subtypes, developmental and neurological manifestations, ocular complications, and metabolic syndrome are interconnected within the overarching framework of diabetes mellitus, underscoring the importance of comprehensive assessment and management in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Other specified conditions related to pregnancy comprise a range of distinct gestational disorders. This category includes renal disorder during pregnancy, peripheral neuropathy during pregnancy, and polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, each representing a separate organ‑system complication that can arise in the context of gestation and is classified under the broader label of other specified pregnancy‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 579
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease entity that encompasses a wide range of related conditions, complications, and associated diagnostic components. Several specific forms of diabetes, including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to the broader category of diabetes, indicating that these neonatal and transient presentations are considered subtypes of the same metabolic disorder. Clinically, diabetes is linked to multiple serious complications. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) is clinically associated with diabetes, reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy. Congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني) is also clinically associated with diabetes, and the procedure of amputation of lower extremity is further linked to this cardiac condition, underscoring the systemic impact of the disease.
